raw_consumer_statement: On XX/XX/year>, I was delayed by security and missed a returning from XXXX to XXXX  on XXXX XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX. I called the agency that originally sold me the ticket and was transferred a few times until they put me on the phone with someone who claimed to be an employee at XXXX XXXX. He told me if I upgraded to a premium ticket ( {$9800.00} ) that it would be completely refunded to me within one to two billing cycles. His emails had XXXX XXXX logos which added credibility. I gave a conditional authorization provided I was entitled to the refund in writing and a confirmation email of the start of the refund process again with XXXX XXXX logo. Two months later, still no refund. I contacted XXXX XXXX and my original seller and none had any record of an employee by the name or of such a refund. I feel so silly now as the fraud by inducement and impersonation is now so obvious. I had been transferred to a scammer. A review of my Citibank credit card shoed XXXX charges on that date. One was from the authentic XXXX XXXX for a ticket at the price of {$1200.00}. The other charge was for {$8500.00} from XXXXwhich add up to {$9800.00}. I am obviously the victim of a fraud that included both inducement and impersonation. However, in the face of such obvious fraud, Citibank is refusing to issue a reversal as they claim their their completed investigation concludes that I accepted responsibility for the disputed charges when I provided my account number.
